Hey guys, JB in the house. Time for your daily motivation slash skills training. We're in module one, straight line certification. This particular motivation corresponds directly to idealistic four, pretty much four on the nose, the end of three, four. We're talking about now what happens with those. You have those three tens lined up. They love your product. They trust and connect with you. They trust the company that stands behind the product, but you still got those tough people like my dad, those high action threshold people who won't buy. And by the way, for those of you who are not on straight line certification, come on. Come on. Get a life. Let's go. Hop a board here. It's a 30 day money back guarantee. Let's roll forward. So here's the takeaway, the big takeaway, right? Is that the biggest mistake that I see in my life is that with this idea of action threshold is people prejudge. You look at someone and say, they have a high action threshold, they don't. You think you could spot it out. Let me just tell you something. You can't. You cannot look at someone. You can't hear them say 10 words. You don't know how high someone's action threshold is until you line up the first three elements of your first three elements. And you know you got them lined up because their responses are like, wow, I love it. And they're still not buying, right? That's when this reveals itself. Why am I telling you this? Because what I don't want you to do, the mistake I see all the time is you people try to lower the action threshold before they've created airtight certainty for the three 10s. Don't do that. Follow the syntax as much as possible. And if you're forced to free form, of course, we have to have flexibility, make sure you always loop back and build those three 10s. Because until you have the three 10s at a really high level, lowering the action threshold, it's hard to do anything for you. Not going to do a lot. It's going to be pretty much a dead-end street for you. So I want you to make sure you take the time to line up those three elements. Don't jump to just, you know, what's the worst thing? Don't do that in the beginning. Don't do that. Wait. A perfect example is when I was releasing the straight line certification, you didn't hear me talk about what's the worst that could possibly happen. And I said, hey, I'll give you a 30-day money back guarantee, right? I didn't say it to the end. Why? Because the reason you're off of someone a 30-day money back guarantee is because if you have someone that's really, really certain, but they have a high action threshold, right? Or sometimes you might say, they're really, really certain, but they might not be quite as certain that it will work for them. Just don't forget that when you talk about certainty. It's like, OK, the certainty works, but will it work for them? So what happens is when you lower the action threshold, those seeds of doubt, it's not like they evaporate. The seeds of doubt remain. What does change, though, is their decision-making strategy. You turn it into someone that will still make a positive, buying decision, despite them still having seeds of doubt. That's the difference. Doesn't change who they are. It just temporarily, or how they feel, it all throws their decision-making strategy in the moment. Of course, you have a crappy product. Well, the 30-day guarantee is going to bite in the butt because they're going to return your product. I was able to do it because I know it's awesome. And I know I'm going to over-deliver. So be careful because you shouldn't be selling crap. Anyway, but the point is most companies sell good stuff, and people are skeptical. So you lower the action threshold at the end. Remember syntax. Action threshold is like, I haven't even gotten it yet, but remember, it's all, I got the set number all. I think it's nine, eight, or nine, or 10 of the syntax.
